company_persona: an AI coding assistant for professional developers
title: The margin squeeze
prompt: You're the PM for Marlow, an AI coding assistant with about 60,000 weekly active developers, sold at a flat $20 per seat per month. Finance just flagged that inference cost per weekly active user has doubled over the last six weeks — from about $1.10 to $2.30 per user per week — while revenue hasn't moved at all. Gross margin on the plan has slid from roughly 75% to 50%, and the CFO wants answers before the board meeting in two weeks. Walk me through how you'd figure out what's going on and what you'd do about it.

hidden_facts.onset: The rise is gradual and compounding — roughly +12% week over week for six weeks. There is no single-day step change anywhere in the cost curve.
hidden_facts.instrumentation: Cost dashboards reconcile to the model provider's invoice, and neither the cost-attribution logic nor the WAU definition changed in this window. The doubling is real dollars.
hidden_facts.decomposition: Requests per active user are up ~1.15x and tokens per request are up ~1.9x. Blended price per token is roughly flat: the provider's 8% price cut was offset by mix shifting toward the frontier model.
hidden_facts.segment_plan: Nearly all of the increase comes from ~9,000 power users on the flat-rate Pro Unlimited plan — their cost per user is up ~4x. The other 51,000 users are roughly flat.
hidden_facts.release: 'Autopilot', an agentic mode that plans a task, edits multiple files, runs tests, and retries on failure, went GA six weeks ago. Power-user adoption has climbed steadily from 3% to 38% of sessions — the adoption curve matches the cost curve.
hidden_facts.usage_mix: An Autopilot task averages ~22 model calls and ~180K tokens, versus ~1.2K tokens for a normal completion or chat turn. Autopilot is about 1% of requests but ~60% of total tokens, and it defaults every step — even trivial ones like renaming a variable — to the frontier model.
hidden_facts.pricing_model: Pricing is flat per-seat with no usage metering or caps, so heavy Autopilot use generates zero incremental revenue. Seats, ARPU, and WAU (~60K) are all flat — no trial surge or traffic-mix change.
hidden_facts.retries_quality: Autopilot's task success rate is 71%; failed tasks auto-retry up to 3 times and every retry is billed. p95 latency is up, but only because agent runs are long — there is no infra regression.
hidden_facts.provider_pricing: The model provider did not raise prices — it actually cut frontier-model per-token prices 8% four weeks ago.
hidden_facts.cache_incident: Prompt-cache hit rate fell from 62% to 20% for about 36 hours three weeks ago during a cache-cluster migration, then fully recovered. It explains under 3% of the six-week increase.

ideal_answer_notes: Confirm the metric is real before diagnosing: check that cost dashboards reconcile to the provider invoice and that neither cost-attribution logic nor the WAU definition changed (they didn't — it's real dollars).
Establish onset shape: a smooth ~12%-per-week compounding rise with no step change — this points to adoption or mix shift, not a one-off deploy, config flip, or provider price change.

Decompose cost per WAU into requests per user x tokens per request x price per token; discover tokens per request (~1.9x) is the dominant mover while blended price per token is flat — killing the 'provider raised prices' hypothesis early.
Segment by plan and cohort: ~9,000 flat-rate Pro Unlimited power users are up ~4x while the other 51,000 users are flat — the problem is concentrated, not systemic.

Ask what shipped in the window: Autopilot went GA six weeks ago and its power-user adoption curve (3% to 38% of sessions) tracks the cost curve; confirm mechanically via token mix — ~1% of requests but ~60% of tokens, frontier model on every step, billed retries at a 71% success rate.

Explicitly dismiss the red herrings with numbers: the cache-migration dip lasted 36 hours and explains under 3% of the increase; rising p95 latency is a symptom of long agent runs, not an infra regression to fix.

Name the business mechanism: flat per-seat pricing with no metering means Autopilot's marginal cost has no marginal revenue — power users now cost roughly $10/week against about $4.60/week of revenue.

Propose mitigations along the cost/latency/quality frontier: route planning and trivial steps to a cheaper model, cap or smart-degrade retries, prompt-cache repo context, set per-task token budgets — each paired with a guardrail (Autopilot task success rate, suggestion acceptance) and validated with offline evals plus an A/B test before rollout.

Address the structural fix: metered Autopilot credits or a usage-based tier for agent tasks, plus a standing margin-per-cohort dashboard and cost-per-task alerting so the next drift is caught in days, not weeks.
extra_checklist: Candidate decomposes cost per active user into volume, tokens per request, and price per token (or an equivalent factorization) before committing to a root cause.
Candidate quantitatively dismisses at least one red herring (the 36-hour cache dip or the provider price change) rather than building their plan around it.
Candidate pairs every proposed cost mitigation with an explicit quality guardrail metric (e.g., Autopilot task success rate or suggestion acceptance) and a validation method.

anchors.strong: Verifies the cost is invoice-real, reads the compounding onset as adoption rather than a config change, decomposes cost per WAU into requests, tokens, and price, and segments straight to the 9K Pro Unlimited cohort. Connects Autopilot's adoption curve to its token mix (1% of requests, ~60% of tokens), quantifies the flat-rate pricing mismatch, numerically dismisses the cache dip and provider-pricing herrings, then proposes model routing, retry caps, and metered pricing with task-success guardrails and A/B validation.
anchors.at_bar: Reaches Autopilot as the driver but needs nudges — asks what shipped before establishing onset or segmenting, or spends a few minutes on the cache incident before letting it go. Decomposition is partial: notices tokens per request rose but doesn't isolate the power-user cohort unprompted. Proposes sensible mitigations like cheaper-model routing or retry caps, but stays vague on quality guardrails and only connects flat revenue to unmetered pricing after prompting.
anchors.weak: Opens with a hypothesis dump — provider raised prices, user growth, an infra bug — without asking about onset shape, segments, or what shipped. Latches onto the cache incident or rising latency and builds a fix plan around it. Never decomposes cost per user, never connects flat revenue to unmetered flat-rate pricing, and jumps to 'switch everything to a cheaper model' with no quality guardrail or validation plan.

teaser: Inference spend per user doubled in six weeks; revenue didn't move an inch.
